steps.title: Find the key signal
steps.content: The author's decisive claim is that population density, though associated with walkability in Tokyo, is just one variable in a larger system: “many factors influence people's decision-making about whether to walk” and “none of these factors in isolation fully explains pedestrian habits in a given city.” The passage groups density with personal preference, perceived safety, and other factors as interdependent elements, not standalone causes.

steps.title: Match the author's view of density
steps.content:
The correct answer must reflect density as one factor among several, not as a solitary or dominant driver.

Choice B states exactly this—density “should be understood as just one of several factors that influence pedestrian activity in Tokyo.” The other three misrepresent the passage:
  • A invents a reversed effect in other cities
  • C ranks density as less important than other factors (unsupported)
  • D flips the causal direction
, treating density as an outcome rather than an input.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
